content.full_text:
Farn Porrrrcal PnacrrcEs ConaMrssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-56ffi . Fax (91 6) 322-0886
August 6,2003
Mike Morrell
8780 19m Street,#232
Alta Loma, CA 9l70l
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. .4.-01089
DearMr. Morrell:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the..Act,).1
QUESTION
May you transfer to your qrrrent committee for California State Senate funds that
are left over from your 1998 congressional race and located in youn federal committee?
CONCLUSION
You may transfer to your curent state Senate committee funds remaining in your
federal congressional committee, subject to the attribution requirements of section g5306
andregulation 18536.
FACTS
In 1998, you considered a run for the 42"d Congressional District and raised
approximately $11,000 for that race. There czrne a point in time when you stopped
campaigning but kept your federal campaign committee open. You spent approximately
$2,100 and approximately $8,900 remains. You have rro* fo.*"d a "om*itiee for a state
Senate race. You request advice on transferring the campaign funds from your federal
campaign committee to your state election committee.
' Gor"*-".,t Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
I 8 1 09- I 8997, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-03-089
Page No. 2
ANALYSIS
The Political Reform Act ("Act') regulates California state and Iocal elections,
but not federal elections. Candidates for election to federal office are governed by the
Federal Election Campaign Act- AccordinglS the Act's definition of "candidate,, in
section 82007 means a candidate for state orlocal elective office and specifically
excludes fbderal candidates:
"candidate" means an individual who is listed on the ballot or who has
qualified to have write-in votes on his or her behalf comted by election
officials, fornomination for or election to any elective ofEce,2 orwho
receives a contribution or makes an exp€nditure or gves his or her consent
for any other person to receive a contribution or *uk. an expenditgre with
a view to bringing about his or her nomination or elwtion to any elective
office, whether or not the specific elective office for which he or she will
seek nomination or election is known at the time the contn'bution is
received or the expenditme is made and whether ornot he or shehas
announced his or her candidacy or filed a declaration ofcandidacy at such
time. "candidate" also includes any officeholder who is tlre subject of a
recall election. An individual who becomes a candidate shall retain his or
her status as a candidate until such tirne as that status is terninated
pursuant to Section 84214. "Candidate" does not include ony person
within the meaning of section 301(b) of the Federal Election campaigt
Act of 1971. " (Emphasis added.)
Because committees controlled by candidates for federal o{fice me goverr-red by
federal election law, we have not keated them * 't""lpi"rrt ,;;in;;;e 
the Act.
Committees controlled by candidates for federal office are viewed as "persons,,3 for
purposes of determining the contribution limits that apply if a federal committee
contributes to a state candidate. (See, e.g., Miller Advice Letter, No. 4-00-2 42; Weems
Advice Letter, No. 4-91-326; and Rffinburgh Advice Letter, No. 4-90-761.) For
example, a federal committee controlled by a United States Senator may conhibute
$3,2004 to your state Senate campaign under section 85301(a), the same amount that any
other individual or entity may contribute to your campaign-
2 "Elective office" means any state, regional, count5i, municipal, district orjudicial oflice which is
filled at an election. (Seetion 82023.)
3 '?erson" means an individual, proprietorship, firm, partnership, joint venture, syndicate,
business trust, company, corporation, limited liability company, association, committee, and any other
organization or group of persons acting in concert. (Section 82047.)
n Thit is based on the originul $:,OOO conkibution limit, as adjusted by Commission regulation for
inflation. Since the contribution limit applies separately to both the pimary and general election, the total
amount any one donor could contribute to a state Senate candidate who succeeds ihrough the primary
election would be $6,400.


File No. A-03-089
Page No. 3
However, you are asking about a kansfer of funds between two of your own
campaign committees. Proposition34 added a specificprovisionpermitting transfers of
funds between a candidate's own "controlled committees." Section 85306(a) states:
"(a) A candidatemay transfer campaignfunds from one conholled
committee to a conholled committee for elective state office5 of the same
candidate- conkibutions transferred shall beatfibuted to specific
contributors using a 'last in, first out' or 'first ir1 first out' accounting
method, and these attributed conkibutions when aggregatedwith all other
contributions from the same contributormaynot exceed the limits set forth
in Section 85301 or 853A2." (Footrote added.)
Proposition 34 specifically permits a candidate to transfer fimds that he or she
may have raised for a local or state race to another state race of the same candidatg
subject to applicable contribution limits- For exarnple, a mayoral candidate could use
funds from his citywide race to a later race for the state Senate, subject to the state
contribution limits.
The intra-candidate transferprovision of Proposition34permits a candidate to
transfer funds between his orher "controlled committees-" A "controlled committee,, is
defined in section 82016(a) as follows:
"controlled committee" means a committee that is controlled directly or
indirectly by a candidate or state measure proponent or that acts jointly
with a candidate, controlled committee, or state measure prcIlonent in-
connection with themaking of expendifures. A candidate or statemeasure
proponent controls a committee if he or she, his or her agent, or any other
committee he or she controls has a significant influence on the actions or
decisions of the committee."
A "controlled committee" is one that is controlled by a "candidate" which is
defined in the Act to exclude federal candidates- Because a fbderal candidate committee
is not considered a "controlled committee" under the Act, section 85306 does not
specifically address the question of transfers from an old federal committee of a state
candidate to a state committee of the same candidate.
To answer your question, we look to the policy expressed in section 85306 and to
the Ninth Circuit Court ofAppeals case Service Employees International (Jnion v. Fatr
Polilical Practtces Commission (9th Cir. 1992) 955 F.2d 1312, cert. den.505 U.S. 1230,
which considered the issue of a candidate moving funds between two of his or her own
campaign committees. The contribution limits enacted byProposition 73 (except those
applicable to special elections) were struck down because the limits were based on a
5 "'Elective state office' means the office of Governor, Lieutenant Governor, Attorney General,
Insurance Commissioner, Controller, Secretary of State, Treasurer, Superintendent of public Instruction,
Member of the Legislature, member elected to the Board of Administration of the public Employees,
Retirement System, and member of the State Board of Equalization.,, (Section g2024.)


File No. A-03-089
Page No. 4
fiscal year rathet than an election cycle, and the court found this would unconstitutionally
discriminate against challengers. (Id. at 1316-1321.) The Ninth Circuit in SEIU afft -"d
that in the absence of valid contribution limits, a candidate may freely transfer funds
between his or her own committees . (Id- at 1322-) The court invalidated a provision of
Proposition 73 that banned transfers between a candidate's o\iln committee;-6
The district court found that limitations on a candidate's transfer of campaign
funds between committees controlled by the same candidate mgst be reviewed unaer ttre
"strict scrutiny''standard applied by the court to limitations on expenditures, rather than
under the less demanding standmd ofreview applied to contributiin limits. Service
Employees International Union v- Foir Political Practices Commission (E.D. Ca- lgg0)
747 F.supp. 580, 591. The Ninth circuit court of Appeals concurred, stating:
*We agree with the district court that the ban on intra-candidate transfers
operates as an expenditure limitation because it limits thepurposes for
which money raised by a candidate may be spent. Expenditme limitations
are subject to strict scrutiny and will be upheld only if they are 'narrowly
tailored to serve a compelling state interest-' Aastin,l lO S.Ct. at 1396.-
(955F.2d 1372,1321)
The inka-candidate transferban invalidated in SEIU didnot specificallyinvolve a
kansfer from a federal to a state committee. Nevertheless, the holding in the case has
been inskuctive. Specifically, ourprior advice in Miller, above, when addressing a
question and circumstances similar to what you presen! found SElUinstnrctive in its
discussion of the constitutionally-imposed limits on the Act's limits to associational and
other constitutional tight" ofpolitical contributors. Miller concluded tha! consistent with
our interpretation of Proposition 73's contribution limits applicable to spgcial elections,
as upheld in SEIU, a state candidate could provide funds from his orher fueral candidate
committee to his or her state controlled-committee, subject to the attribution requirernents
of section 85306.
Although the intra-candidate transferprovisions of section 85306 do not expressly
apply to your facts, for constitutional reasons similar to those articulated by the court in
SEIU, we advise, as we did in Miller, that these provisions are to be followed with
respect to movernents of funds from your federal congressional committee to your state
Senate committee. Specifically, funds from your old federal congressional committee
may be provided to your state Senate committee, subject to the athibution rules of section
85306 and regulation 18536 (copy enclosed). This means that funds must be attributed to
the individual donors to your federal congressional committee and each such conkibution
may only be provided to your state Senate committee if it would not cause a donor to
your state Senate committee to exceed the applicable contribution limit under section
85301 (a).
u Theo section 85304 stated: 'Ttro candidate for elective office or committee conholled by that
candidate or candidates for elective office shall hansfer any contribution to any other candidate for elective
office- Transfers of funds between candidates or their controlled committees are prohibited.',


a
File No. A-03-089
Page No. 5
If you have any other questions regarding this rnatter, please contact me at (916)
322-566A.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Cormsel
By: ffiv$
Counsel, l-eg?l Division
KLGjg

QUESTION: May you transfer to your qrrrent committee for California State Senate funds that
are left over from your 1998 congressional race and located in youn federal committee?

CONCLUSION: You may transfer to your curent state Senate committee funds remaining in your
federal congressional committee, subject to the attribution requirements of section g5306
andregulation 18536.
